narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 51.3% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4, which is below the typical student's 5.71, and your Year 3 decile of 3, even lower than the average 5.53. Your LSAT score of 35 also falls short of the usual 36.7, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is very close to the average 3.21. As an upper-middle income male student identifying as White and planning full-time studies—traits common in the applicant pool, like 56% male, 84% White, and 93% full-time—the model weighed these details against patterns from thousands of past students.

The strongest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 decile, as lower performance there signals challenges in sustaining strong grades. Next in influence came your Year 1 decile, reinforcing that early academic struggles mattered a lot. Your LSAT score ranked third, suggesting it had a notable but slightly lesser push toward the failure prediction compared to your school deciles. Family income followed, with your upper-middle bracket contributing similarly, then your undergraduate GPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender in descending order of impact—all nudging the model slightly more toward expecting a bar failure than success.

In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, your deciles are in the lower half of the distribution, and your LSAT is below the norm that correlates with first-try success. While your GPA and demographics align closely with averages, they weren't enough to offset the academic red flags. This close call at 51.3% shows you're not far off, unlike students with much higher failure risks.

To improve your odds next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above 37, as scores are highly influential and retakes are common. Build a stronger recent academic story through postgraduate courses or certifications showing grade improvement beyond your prior deciles. Consider part-time options if they fit better, though full-time is standard—more importantly, seek bar prep resources early, like study groups or tutors, tailored for students with similar starting points. With targeted effort on these, you could flip the prediction in your favor for future applications. Keep pushing; many overcome these hurdles.
